sections.heading: Traditional use declined even as overall use held steady
sections.body: Among currently married women, any contraceptive use was almost unchanged at 58.6 percent in 2025, compared with 58.3 percent in 2022. The traditional-method share moved in the other direction, falling from 16.5 percent to 14.1 percent. Withdrawal remained the dominant traditional method, but it also declined from 13.4 percent to 11.8 percent. The national picture is therefore not a broad retreat from contraception, but a smaller role for traditional methods within a nearly stable total.

sections.heading: The highest regional levels sit in Luzon and Eastern Visayas
sections.body: Traditional-method use among currently married women was highest in Ilocos Region at 22.3 percent, followed by Eastern Visayas at 20.4 percent and CALABARZON at 18.1 percent. Central Luzon and Bicol Region were also above the national level, both at 17.2 percent. These higher-use regions are not all adjacent, but they do cluster around places where withdrawal remains a large part of the method mix. In Ilocos Region, for example, withdrawal alone accounted for 20.2 percent.

sections.heading: Several regions moved away from traditional methods sharply
sections.body: The national decline was modest, but some regional shifts were much larger. BARMM fell from 14.7 percent in 2022 to 5.1 percent in 2025, while Bicol Region declined from 25.7 percent to 17.2 percent and Central Visayas from 16.3 percent to 8.4 percent. MIMAROPA also dropped from 14.5 percent to 8.6 percent. These changes suggest that the traditional-method trend is being pulled by a few large regional moves, not by uniform movement across the country.

sections.heading: Low-use regions are mostly in Mindanao and Central Visayas
sections.body: The lowest 2025 traditional-method levels were in BARMM at 5.1 percent, Cagayan Valley at 6.9 percent, Central Visayas at 8.4 percent, MIMAROPA at 8.6 percent, and Negros Island Region at 9.0 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ was also below the national level at 9.4 percent. Several Mindanao regions were below 12 percent, including Northern Mindanao, SOCCSKSARGEN, and Caraga, while Davao Region was also below the national level at 12.2 percent. The regional spread runs from 5.1 percent to 22.3 percent, so geography matters more than small national movement would imply.

sections.heading: Age differences are present, but not dramatic
sections.body: Traditional-method use among currently married women was lowest at ages 15 to 19, at 8.7 percent, and highest at ages 35 to 39, at 15.4 percent. Most adult age groups from 20 to 49 were packed within a fairly narrow band, from 12.1 percent to 15.4 percent. The sharper age signal is that younger married women rely less on traditional methods than women in their late twenties through forties. Withdrawal accounts for most of the traditional-method share in every age group.

sections.heading: Traditional methods peak before the largest-family categories
sections.body: By number of living children, traditional-method use was highest among currently married women with one to two children, at 16.7 percent. It was lower among women with three to four children at 11.9 percent and nearly the same among women with five or more children at 12.0 percent. Women with no living children were also lower, at 9.0 percent. This pattern is different from total contraceptive use, which was highest among women with three to four children at 70.9 percent.

sections.heading: Urban married women used traditional methods more than rural married women
sections.body: Urban currently married women had higher traditional-method use than rural currently married women, 15.8 percent versus 12.2 percent. The same table shows rural women with higher modern-method use, 47.2 percent compared with 42.1 percent in urban areas. That contrast keeps the residence pattern from being a simple difference in overall contraceptive use, because total use was similar in rural and urban areas. It is more about method mix than whether contraception was being used at all.

sections.heading: Post-secondary and college groups stand out on traditional methods
sections.body: Traditional-method use was highest among currently married women with post-secondary education at 18.4 percent and college education at 16.6 percent. The post-secondary group also showed a large increase from 12.1 percent in 2022, while college remained high despite declining from 19.0 percent. Lower-education groups did not follow the same pattern: women with Grades 1 to 6 were at 9.3 percent, and those with no education were at 7.6 percent. This is a method-mix pattern, not a simple education gradient in overall contraceptive use.

sections.heading: Traditional use is lowest in the poorest quintile, not the richest
sections.body: Across wealth quintiles, traditional-method use was lowest among currently married women in the lowest quintile, at 10.4 percent. The middle, fourth, and highest quintiles were all higher, at 16.0 percent, 15.0 percent, and 15.9 percent. The highest quintile did not have the highest overall contraceptive use, but it still had a relatively high traditional-method share. This again points to method mix rather than simple access or use levels.

sections.heading: Sexually active unmarried women show a sharper decline
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, traditional-method use declined from 17.6 percent in 2022 to 11.7 percent in 2025. Withdrawal drove most of that level and most of that decline, falling from 15.0 percent to 10.4 percent. Modern-method use rose from 23.8 percent to 26.2 percent over the same period. This denominator is separate from currently married women, but it shows the same broad shift away from traditional methods.

sections.heading: The unmarried residence gap narrowed, but rural use remains higher
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, rural traditional-method use was 14.4 percent in 2025, compared with 10.3 percent in urban areas. Both declined sharply from 2022, when the figures were 20.8 percent for rural women and 16.0 percent for urban women. Withdrawal again accounts for nearly all of the traditional-method share in both places. The residence gap remained, but the decline was visible in both urban and rural settings.

takeaways: Traditional-method use among currently married women declined to 14.1 percent in 2025 even though total contraceptive use stayed nearly unchanged.
Regional variation is large, with Ilocos Region and Eastern Visayas high and BARMM, Cagayan Valley, Central Visayas, and MIMAROPA low.
Withdrawal is the main traditional method across married and sexually active unmarried women.
Sexually active unmarried women also moved away from traditional methods, falling from 17.6 percent in 2022 to 11.7 percent in 2025.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
